Commentary: The fricative β is in contrast with approximant w, but has no contrasting voiceless segment. The velar fricative x has a very limited distribution. The underlying affricate ts has fricative realizations.
No fricative realization is reported for the underlying affricates
No fricative realization is reported for the underlying affricates; in Kanamari, but not in Katukina, the fricative h occurs in underlyingly in codas.
The glottal h has both voiced and nasalized realizations; the coronal contrast is between lamino-alveolar s and apical-post-alveolar s̺ (the latter having [ʃ] as an allophone)

The glottal h is restricted to syllable codas and has allophones [ç] and [x] conditioned by the place (palatal or dorsal) of the preceding vowel; note that Carlin (2004) considers ɸ to be an independent phoneme. This is tied to dialectal differences within Tiriyó and interesting sound changes.

Although both sources agree on the number of consonants and the number of fricatives, Tavares (2005) analyzes the single fricative as h, while Camargo (1996) postulates a single phoneme s. I have followed the latter, simply because this seems to be the etymological situation, h being historically derived.
The glottal h is classified as an approximant in the source; the fricative s also has affricate realizations.

Since the affricate t͡ʃ has an uncertain phonological status but is clearly historically secondary, the same criterion of historical primacy has been followed as in the case of the Wayana fricative. The Ikpeng affricate was excluded from the inventory of contrastive Ikpeng segments.
The fricative hʷ may be realized as ɸ; the approximant w has a fricative realization β preceding a front vowel.

All the underlying affricates (ts, tsʰ and dz) have optional fricative realizations. Kulina has an apparently rare aspirated fricative realization. The approximant w has a labiodental fricative (free) allophone [v] preceding i and e. Note that while no underlying fricative is posited, a “rare phoneme” s is considered as an innovation of the Madihá branch, though one restricted to sound-symbolic words and loans.
The only voiced stop is b; the post-alveolar ʃ is considered a marginal phoneme by the author, but since it occurs in inherited/etymological words, it is counted here.
The affricate t͡ʃ has a fricative allophone ʃ, and the glide w has an allophone β.
Earlier sources on Iquito treat ʃ as an allophone of s in the context of a following front vowel.
The glottal fricative h triggers contextual nasalization and is associated with morphophonemic adjustments that yield surface aspirated obstruents and devoiced sonorants.
The voiced fricative ʐ seems to be possibly classifiable as a rhotic instead.
The voiced stops b and g have fricative allophones
The approximant w can be realized as β preceding a front vowel i or e; both ɸ and x seem to be recent developments of *pʰ and *kʰ, respectively.
The glottal h has a limited distribution and is undergoing loss; the approximant w has a [β] realization in intervocalic position.
The glottal h has a limited distribution, being restricted to syllable-final (but not word-final) position.

Fricatives: f s ɬ ʃ x h
Commentary: There is a single voiced stop, d. Note that the author of the single reference work on the language classifies the lateral fricative ɬ as part of the liquid series, outside of the fricative series. It has been included as a fricative here.
The fricative h has an allophone ɸ preceding u, and the approximant w is realized as β preceding i.
The glottal fricative h is restricted to ideophones and a few loans; the velar fricative ɣ is not clearly contrastive and, in any case, stems from the lenition of k.
The approximant w has a free variant β.
The voiced stops b and g, as well as the voiceless palatal stop c, all have freely occurring fricative allophones in prosodically weak contexts.
The glottal fricative h is being lost in the speech of younger speakers; it is possible that g is becoming an independent phoneme.

The approximant w has a fricative allophone β when preceding a front vowel; surface [ɣ] is considered a realization of g, but note that [g] has more limited distribution, and the fricative might be a better characteristic allophone; some speakers have s realized as [t͡s] instead.
The fricative v also has [β] as a possible realization.
The fricative ʃ is extremely infrequent; h can be optionally inserted in some medial codas; hw might be another possible marginal phoneme.
The single voiced fricative β has a realization range including approximant and stop realizations, but the fricative realization is said to be the most frequent (Valenzuela 2003:98).
The approximant w is often realized as [v] in word-initial position (Stenzel 2004:68).
The glottal h is included by Hanson (2010) in the series of nasal consonants, due to its rhinoglottophilia effects.
The palatal approximant j has optional fricative realizations.
Affricate t͡ʃ has an optional realization as ʃ.
The voiceless counterpart of z, s, is a marginal phoneme incorporated in the speech of younger individuals
According to Osborn (1966), w has a fricative allophone β in medial position.

The phonological status of the glottal stop ʔ is uncertain; the fricative h has an uncertain status and may be predictable; the fricative ɹ̝ is described as a ‘voiced post-alveolar’ fricative, “the same sound of r in the dr cluster in English <dream>”.
The labial fricative is symbolized as <f>, but described as bilabial (Sakel 2004:29)
The fricative <f> occurs only in Spanish loans; β is seemingly contrastive, but stronger evidence is still needed.
Note that although the language has voiced fricatives while lacking voiced stops, it seems like β and ʝ could be approximants; overall number of fricatives somewhat inflated due to βʲ and hʲ.

ç can appear only in morpheme-final position and is realized as [ʲh]; palatal stop c has fricative realizations (Epps 2008:51-2); the approximant w is pronounced as a fricative β before front vowels (Epps 2008:61).
The fricative f is restricted to morpheme-initial position, it can be realized as [f] or [ɸ] depending on the clanic affiliation of the speaker (Bolaños 2016:46).
Both t͡s and d͡z have fricative allophones.
The fricative [β] may occur as a free variant of w; the single voiced stop is uvular ɢ, which seems to reflect a rhotic. The single voiced fricative ʐ seems to reflect a rhotic.
The glottal h has a limited distribution and also triggers nasalization.
The glottal fricative h has a velar allophone [x] before consonants (Michael et al. 2013:317); the fricative ʃ is limited to very few words (Michael et al. 2013:318),

Although Borgman (1990) postulates a t͡s – s contrast in Venezuelan Sanumá, he notes that the contrast is very limited, notably in Brazilian Sanumá (described in Autuori 2013), and employs a single symbol <s> in the Sanumá practical orthography.
The aspirated stop pʰ has an allophone ɸ in free variation.
Both s and h have palatal allophones following i or j.

There is an alternation between word-final p and β, as well as m ~ β, which is described by the source as a case of allophony triggered by a following vowel (or an intervocalic context), but must be morphophonemic alternation.

The labial fricative β has [v b β w] allophones (Terhart 2022); the fricatives ʃ and ʂ were incorporated via loans from Bésiro, and h triggers nasalization of a following vowel (rhinoglottophilia) (Terhart 2022:50).
Fricative v includes [β w b] realizations
The fricatives s and ʃ are neutralized to the postalveolar realization preceding a high front vowel.

The labial stop p is implemented as ɸ when vowel syncope makes it adjacent to a following h; in the same context, k appears as x; fricatives s ʃ and t͡s and t͡ʃ are neutralized, showing either only fricative or only affricate realizations, in some contexts. The glottal fricative h also triggers nasalization (rhinoglottophilia).
The author discussses language-specific criteria for classifying h among the obstruents, not the sonorants. Note that h is also a default consonant inserted in different morphophonological contexts.
The fricative β occurs as a lenited, intervocalic allophone of the stop consonant b.
Note that v and f alternate freely in word-initial position.
The approximant j can become a fricative ʝ before high front vowels.
Both s and j have fricative, affricate and stop realizations; the fricative s is palatalized before e (Landaburu 2023:129)
The glide w is realized as β preceding i in some dialects of the language.
The approximant w has a fricative realization β when preceded and followed by the same vowel.
The stop g has a fricative realization ɣ.
The approximant j is often realized as [z] and [ð].

Although β is lacking, the author posits a contrast between two approximants w and β, offering acoustic evidence that the latter lacks frication in its production; the rhotic ɾ may be realized as a retroflex fricative ʐ.

Everett 2007 suggests that two voiced stops, b and d, are in the process of becoming indepenent phonemes in the language; the author notes that, while h clearly patterns as a consonant (and has a distribution similar to that of s), it has no clear constriction, and sounds like voiceless version of adjacent vowels.

The stop consonant k has a realization [ɣ] in intervocalic external sandhi contexts; the language has no underlying fricatives, only the affricate t͡ʃ. Note, however, that this segment, although phonologically classified as a stop by the author of the source, does not behave like a stop in all aspects, both distributionally and in terms of a phonetic lengthening rule to which stops are subject (Aragon 2014:85).
The stop p can be realized as [ɸ] before back round vowels.
The phonological status of the glottal consonants h and ʔ is unclear, and the author proposes that these are autosegmental glottal elements rather than segmental elements.
The glottal h has a limited distribution, never appearing word-initially, and has a quality that is identical to that of flanking vowels.
Both affricates t͡s and d͡ʒ have fricative realizations in many contexts and, in particular, in the speech of younger speakers.

The fricative s is of reduced frequency and, according to Bostrom (1998), is in the process of being lost in the language (note that most s in other Tukanoan languages match Tatuyo h in cognate morphemes); Gómez-Imbert (1980), which gives the consonant as a palatal stop c, also notes its reduced frequency. The approximant w appears as [β] preceding a high front vowel.
The stop k may surface as ɣ between two vowels ɨ in casual speech.
The affricate t͡ʃ has a fricative allophone ʃ and the approximant w may be realized as β and j has ʃ and ʒ as allophones as well.
The approximant w has a fricative allophone β when preceding a front vowel.
The consonant β is an approximant, with an open articulation without friction.
Yukuna has a whole set of contrasting voiced and voiceless sonorants, which yields a relatively large segmental inventory.
The approximant j has optional fricative realizations [ʝ].
The voiced stops are implosives; the consonant inventory does not include approximant vocoids, which are considered to be positional allophones of high vowels.

The large consonantal inventory derives from Kroeker’s analysis of aspirated and glottalized consonants as independent phonemes. Lowie (1999) derives a much smaller segmental inventory, as he analyzes aspirated and glottalized consonants as clusters.
The voiced stops are implosives, so the inventory can be considered to lack voiced stops, if voicing is non-contrastive.
One of the consonantal segments postulated for Nomatsiguenga is a ‘unspecified nasal N’, of uncertain status.
Nanti seems to be the only Campan language where rhinoglottophilia is attested; the approximant j has [ʒ] as one of its allophones; the bilabial voiced stop b has [β] as one of its allophones.
The fricative h is a very marginal phoneme found only in a couple of words, mostly anthroponyms.
The western dialect of Bakairi lacks the voiced fricatives. The system with voiced fricatives is reconstructed for Proto-Bakairi.
The bilabial fricative ɸ is said to be retained in a few words only, being generally merged with h.
